terial improvement in their condi-
tions. They want and will have
them now and in the continually

sort to politics whenever it will
bring us advantage, whenever it

step further"


In speaking of labor's grievence
against the party in power and par
ticularly the Republicans in con-
gress, Mr.. Gompers said:


what did organized labor get
from congress? 1t was interested
in several bills; it argued, present
ed data, attended hearings, when
the session came to an end, what
was the net result of this work?
There was no result. The eight
hour bill, the anti-injunction bill,
and other bills tending to the pro-
lection of the rights and lives of
men, women and children, had
again been juggled with, but none
of them passed and there was no
prospect of any satisfactory action
on any of them at the next session.
The game is an old one"


1t is the boast of Mr.. Gompers
that union labor has already dem
onstrated its ability to make or I'M
make the regular party candidates
and has long list Of laws-good
laws-to it's credit. Said Mr.. Com
pers:


"Over 80 years ago the trade I'M
ionists secured the establishment
of the bureau of labor statistics in
Massachusetts. 1t was the first
bureau of the kind ever esta blsh-
ed in the world. Since then bur-
eaus of labor statistics have been
established in many states.


ut was the demand of our feder-
alton which compelled many states
to establish inspectors of factories,
shops. mills, mines and tenements.


AT the demand of organized la
Dor, child labor laws have been
placed upon the statute books of
our several states. 1t is our move
ment which seeks for the final and
absolute abolition of child labor.


ut was our federation which Sc
cured to the seamen, for the first
time in history, the right of owner
ship in himself; the right to quit
his work when his vessel was in
safe harbor.
